text:
Thank you. Shall we bow our heads just a moment while we're standing. Our heavenly Father, we come to Thee tonight with the humbleness of our hearts, to express to You the gratitude for Your goodness and kindness to us, we who are unworthy people, and one time alienated from God, without hope, without mercy, without Christ, and Jesus died for us to redeem us back to the fellowship and relationship with God. And now we're sons and daughters of God. It does not yet appear what we shall be finally at the end, but we know we are going to have a body just like His, for we shall see Him as He is.
We pray that You'll bless this group tonight exceedingly abundantly for all their goodness, and their mercies, and their thoughts, and love tokens. And tonight as it seems to be with other meetings allotted that we must journey on to another city, as our Lord said, "I must go to another city and preach the Gospel."
But O God, someday we're going to meet when we will never part again, in that city where there'll be no night services. It'll be day, for the Lamb is the light. And we long to see Him, who is the light of the world and the light of that great city.
And bless us tonight in our efforts. And help Your poor, tired servant, Lord, with not much voice left to speak to the purchase of Your blood. But believing that You will meet us here and would help us, and we will praise You for it; for we ask it in the name of Thy beloved Son, the Lord Jesus. Amen. You may be seated.
text_no:
La oss bøye hodene et øyeblikk mens vi står. Vår himmelske Far, vi kommer til Deg i kveld med ydmyke hjerter for å uttrykke vår takknemlighet for Din godhet og vennlighet mot oss, vi som er uverdige mennesker, en gang fremmede for Gud, uten håp, uten nåde, uten Kristus. Jesus døde for å gjenløse oss tilbake til fellesskapet og forholdet med Gud. Og nå er vi Guds sønner og døtre. Det er ennå ikke åpenbart hva vi endelig skal bli, men vi vet at vi skal ha en kropp lik Hans, for vi skal se Ham som Han er.
Vi ber om at Du velsigner denne gruppen i kveld overmåte for all deres godhet, nåde, tanker og kjærlighetsgaver. I kveld, som med andre møter, ser det ut til at vi må reise videre til en annen by, slik som vår Herre sa: "Jeg må gå til en annen by og forkynne Evangeliet."
Men, O Gud, en dag skal vi møtes og aldri mer skilles, i den byen hvor det ikke vil være noen nattmøter. Det vil være dag, for Lammet er lyset. Og vi lengter etter å se Ham som er verdens lys og lyset i den store byen.
Velsign oss i kveld i våre anstrengelser, og hjelp Din slitne tjener, Herre, med lite stemme igjen til å tale til Dem Du har gjenløst med Ditt blod. Men vi tror at Du vil møte oss her og hjelpe oss, og vi vil prise Deg for det; for vi ber om det i Din elskede Sønn, Herren Jesu navn. Amen. Dere kan sette dere ned.

text:
I wish to express my most sincere thanks to Dr. Lee Vayle and to the cooperating pastors of the city and around about, who has dismissed their services for this campaign. It's with gratitude and a heart of love that I pray that God will bless you exceedingly abundantly in doing this, and for your real Christian feeling towards the work of God. I do thank you for being as your coworker in the kingdom.
Then I wish to express to this audience your faithfulness of coming each night and packing in and filling up the auditorium and so forth each night, coming maybe many miles through the snow and slick roads to come. You never come just to be seen. You come for what good that the Lord will give you. And I thank you for it, my gallant brother and sister and friends of Christ.
text_no:
Jeg vil uttrykke min dypeste takknemlighet til Dr. Lee Vayle og de samarbeidsvillige pastorene i byen og omegn, som har avlyst sine møter for å støtte denne kampanjen. Med takknemlighet og et kjærlig hjerte ber jeg om at Gud rikelig velsigner dere for deres handlinger og deres ekte kristne engasjement for Guds verk. Jeg takker dere for at dere er mine medarbeidere i Guds rike.
Deretter vil jeg uttrykke min takknemlighet til dette publikum for deres trofasthet ved å komme hver kveld, fylle auditoriet og reise kanskje mange mil gjennom snø og glatte veier. Dere kommer ikke bare for å bli sett, men for å motta de gode tingene Herren gir dere. Jeg takker dere, mine tapre brødre og søstre og Kristi venner.

text:
Trusting that some glorious day that we won't have to travel like this to meet each other. But wanting to make an appointment with each of you, that after it's all over, how I would like to go home with each of you tonight, stay all night with you, get up in the morning and talk of the things of God, and the fellowship that we could have around the Word of God in your home. And be there, and eat with you, and fellowship, I just can't do it; because the harvest is ripe and the time is close at hand. So I must be going on. But someday I want an appointment with you, that we'll sit down in another kingdom, the kingdom of the Father, on the other side. And then we'll just have forever to be together.
And I certainly covet the prayers of each of you people. Now, it won't be long, if God willing, I will be going across to the foreign soil, Africa, India, through Asia, Japan. We're looking for the greatest religious meeting that's ever been in the history of the world when we come to Japan. There's only two thousand Full Gospel people there. And they are worshippers of Buddha and their god is defeated. They're a great nation without a god. So I want to take Jesus to them.
text_no:
Jeg stoler på at en herlig dag kommer når vi ikke lenger må reise slik for å møte hverandre. Men jeg ønsker å gjøre en avtale med hver av dere, om at når alt er over, hvor gjerne jeg ville dratt hjem med dere i kveld, bli over natten, stå opp om morgenen og snakke om Guds ting, og ha fellesskap rundt Guds Ord i deres hjem. Å være der, spise og ha fellesskap med dere, men jeg kan rett og slett ikke gjøre det fordi høsten er moden og tiden er nær. Derfor må jeg gå videre. Men en dag vil jeg ha en avtale med dere, at vi skal sette oss ned i et annet rike, Faderens rike, på den andre siden. Da vil vi ha evigheten for å være sammen.
Jeg setter høyt på deres bønner, hver og en av dere. Om ikke lenge, hvis Gud vil, drar jeg til utenlandsk jord, Afrika, India, gjennom Asia, Japan. Vi ser frem til det største religiøse møtet noensinne i verdenshistorien når vi kommer til Japan. Det er bare to tusen Full Gospel-mennesker der. De er Buddha-tilbedere, og deres gud er beseiret. De er en stor nasjon uten en gud. Så jeg vil ta med Jesus til dem.

text:
And now, may I ask you to do this for me? Way overseas, and you know, in the places and things when I'm over there… And the witch doctors and so forth are challenging the Gospel, and I have to stand there, and the winds are blowing hot, can I depend on the people of Lima and around about to be praying for me? Will you do it? Thank you. Thank you much. And someday we'll meet and God will reward you, before that even, for doing it.
I was certainly thankful to the board that let us have this beautiful little auditorium here. It's a pretty little place. And it's so marvelously arranged, and the acoustics seems to be so good. And the audience is drawed right up to you. I like that. I like the close feeling. Some of the best meetings I ever had was in cottage prayer meetings, where we was sitting right around each other, fellowshipping. And this little auditorium, for a place of its size, produces that. And I think it's a lovely little place. I was cutting a picture out that I found in the paper, to take with me.
And to the custodians and for all their kindness, may the Lord richly bless you, gentlemen and brethren.
text_no:
Og nå, kan jeg be dere om en tjeneste? Når jeg er langt borte, helt over havet, og befinner meg i situasjoner hvor heksedoktorer og andre utfordrer Evangeliet, og jeg må stå der mens de varme vindene blåser, kan jeg da stole på at folket i Lima og omegn ber for meg? Vil dere gjøre det? Tusen takk. Takk skal dere ha. En dag vil vi møtes, og Gud vil belønne dere, til og med før da, for at dere gjør det.
Jeg er virkelig takknemlig til styret som lot oss bruke dette vakre lille auditoriet her. Det er et pent lite sted, og det er så fantastisk arrangert, med god akustikk. Publikum er tett på, og det liker jeg. Jeg liker den nære følelsen. Noen av de beste møtene jeg har hatt, var på husmøter hvor vi satt tett sammen og hadde felleskap. Dette lille auditoriet, i sin størrelse, gir den samme opplevelsen. Jeg synes det er et koselig sted. Jeg klippet ut et bilde jeg fant i avisen for å ta med meg.
Og til vaktmestrene og for all deres vennlighet, må Herren rikelig velsigne dere, herrer og brødre.

text:
Then I was just asked … or, said to me tonight by my son, just a few minutes ago, that they had taken a love offering for me. Well, I told Dr. Vayle, "You shouldn't do that." Just to put it in on expenses. And they said, "The expenses was already met." Now, I want to thank you kindly. If there's anything I've tried to do is keep away from money. For I was born poor, and my people are poor. And that's the way I want to be: like I am now. And I never took an offering in my life, myself, never took one up in my life.
I remember one time at home I was going to take an offering. We were poor people, and I… You know when you get to a place you can't make ends meet? Did you ever get to that place? I do all the time. So I was working, and I said to the wife, "You know, honey, I'm going over and take up an offering tonight."
She said, "I'm going to watch you do it." And so she sat back in the room. And now, not as the people…
text_no:
Min sønn sa til meg i kveld, for noen få minutter siden, at de hadde tatt opp en kjærlighetsgave for meg. Jeg sa til Dr. Vayle: "Dere burde ikke gjort det." Han svarte: "Utgiftene er allerede dekket." Jeg vil gjerne takke dere hjertelig. Hvis det er en ting jeg har forsøkt å unngå, så er det penger. Jeg ble født fattig, og mine folk er fattige. Slik ønsker jeg å forbli: som jeg er nå. Jeg har aldri selv tatt opp en offergave i mitt liv.
Jeg husker en gang hjemme da jeg vurderte å ta opp en offergave. Vi var fattige, og du vet hvordan det er når du ikke får endene til å møtes? Har du noen gang vært i den situasjonen? Det er jeg hele tiden. Jeg sa til min kone: "Vet du, kjære, i kveld skal jeg ta opp en offergave."
Hun svarte: "Jeg skal følge med og se hvordan du gjør det." Så satt hun seg tilbake i rommet. Og nå, ikke slik folk...

text:
I pastored there seventeen years without one penny, not because they didn't want to pay me, because I was young, able to work, so I… I'd do it right now if I could just wait on my ministry and be with them. And I love to work.
And I went over to take up the offering, and I said… We didn't have any offering plate, so I said, "Folks, I'm kind of hit a little place, I just can't make the ends meet. I'm going to take a little offering. Somebody got some nickels that you'd like to help me." And I said, "Brother Wisehart (We called him Uncle Jim.), get my hat, will you?" He went and got my hat.
And I looked right down in front of me, and a little old lady (She's in glory tonight)… Remember those little aprons with the pockets on the inside. Did you ever see one? What part of Kentucky you from now, see. And so, it's just a little apron, and she picks out some of these little pocketbooks and snaps on the top, and beginning to reach down for those nickels. Oh, I just couldn't do it, see. Oh, I said, "I was just teasing you. I didn't mean it." And my wife looked at me.
text_no:
Jeg var pastor der i sytten år uten å motta en eneste krone, ikke fordi menigheten ikke ønsket å betale meg, men fordi jeg var ung og i stand til å arbeide selv. Jeg ville fortsatt gjort det om jeg kunne fokusere på min tjeneste og være med dem. Jeg elsker å arbeide.
Da jeg skulle ta opp kollekten, merket jeg at vi ikke hadde noen kollektbøsse. Jeg sa: "Folkens, jeg har støtt på et lite problem og klarer ikke å få endene til å møtes. Jeg vil ta opp en liten kollekt. Er det noen som har noen mynter de vil hjelpe meg med?" Jeg sa: "Bror Wisehart (Vi kalte ham Onkel Jim.), kan du hente hatten min?" Han gikk og hentet hatten.
Jeg så rett foran meg, og en liten, eldre dame (Hun er i herlighet nå)... Husker dere de små forkleene med lommer på innsiden? Har dere sett et slikt? Hvilken del av Kentucky kommer du fra? Hun hadde et lite forkle og tok frem en liten pung med en lukning på toppen. Hun begynte å lete etter noen mynter. Åh, det kunne jeg bare ikke gjøre. Jeg sa: "Jeg bare tullet med dere. Jeg mente det ikke." Min kone så på meg.

text:
And you know, there was an old man used to follow the meeting. Many of you might've seen him. You see him in the meeting. He had a long beard. His name was Ryan, John Ryan. How many ever knowed John Ryan? Well, my, sure. "Partings leave behind us…" And the old man… I used to get on him about wearing his beard. He was a movie star, if you all didn't know that. He was once a movie star. He rode the famous Garland in the Ringling, Barnum and Bailey Circus. And was a Catholic and was converted into the Christian religion and the apostolic faith. And he's gone to glory now, at eighty-something years old.
And he rode an old bicycle down and gave it to me from Dowagiac, Michigan. Oh, it was in bad shape. But I went down, and took ten cents, and bought me a can of paint, and painted it all up, and sold it; got enough money to pay the bill off, and I didn't have to take the offering after all. So I never take an offering.
text_no:
En gamel mann pleide å følge møtene. Mange av dere har kanskje sett ham. Han hadde langt skjegg og het John Ryan. Hvor mange kjente til John Ryan? Vel, ganske mange, tror jeg. "Avskjeder etterlater seg…" Den gamle mannen… Jeg pleide å erte ham for å bære skjegg. Han var en gang filmstjerne, hvis dere ikke visste det. Han hadde tidligere vært filmstjerne og red den berømte Garland i Ringling, Barnum and Bailey Circus. Han var katolikk, men ble omvendt til den kristne religion og den apostoliske tro. Nå har han gått til herlighet, i en alder av over åtti år.
Han syklet ned på en gammel sykkel og ga den til meg fra Dowagiac, Michigan. Den var i dårlig stand. Men jeg gikk og brukte ti cent på en boks maling, malte den opp, og solgte den. Jeg fikk nok penger til å betale regningen, og slapp å ta opp kollekt likevel. Så jeg tar aldri opp kollekt.

text:
I wish that this didn't have to be done, but I know that that's a portion of your living. I know that you're poor people like I am, and perhaps, maybe, that might have to feed the baby [unclear words]. And I realize that. But friends, to the best of my knowledge---I promise you---that it'll not go for foolishness, to the best of my knowledge to the kingdom of God. And may God bring it back to you, multiplied, heaped up, running down … shaken down a hundred-fold, is my prayer.
Now, how many people is here from out of town? Let's see your hands. Say, is there anybody here from Lima? Lima people raise your hands. Oh, yes. You're here. I was just such a [unclear words]. We're happy to be in Lima, aren't we, outsiders? Amen. We think you're wonderful. We think you've got some fine ministers, and some fine makings, and just a lovely city. And we pray that God will bless you exceedingly abundantly.
text_no:
Jeg skulle ønske at dette ikke måtte gjøres, men jeg vet at det er en del av livets realiteter. Jeg vet at dere er fattige folk, slik som jeg, og kanskje må dere bruke dette til å mate babyen [uklare ord]. Jeg forstår det. Men venner, etter beste evne lover jeg dere at dette ikke vil gå til dårlige formål, men til Guds rike. Må Gud gi dere tilbake mangedobbelt, overfylt, presset ned og rennende over, er min bønn.
Hvor mange her er fra andre byer? La oss se hendene deres. Er det noen her fra Lima? Løft hendene, dere fra Lima. Å ja, dere er her. Vi er glad for å være i Lima, ikke sant, tilreisende? Amen. Vi synes dere er fantastiske. Vi mener dere har noen flotte forkynnere, gode forutsetninger og en vakker by. Vi ber om at Gud vil velsigne dere rikelig og overflodsrikt.

text:
And now, tonight I am very hoarse. Usually a minister'd just as soon preach about once a day. Did you know that preaching is harder on you than most any kind of a manual labor that you could do? I've seen that estimated and broken down, and I believe that it is compared… I think that twenty minutes of preaching under inspiration takes more from your physical body than eight hours of a sledge hammer, see. That's how hard it is. That's inspirational preaching.
So we ask tonight… We thank you, and if I've left anybody… Oh, the organist and pianist… There's just so many of you. And today I was just speaking to different ones in places.
text_no:
I kveld er jeg svært hes. Vanligvis foretrekker en forkynner å preke én gang om dagen. Visste du at det å preke er mer krevende enn de fleste typer manuelt arbeid? Jeg har sett det vurdert og analysert, og det anslås at tjue minutter med inspirert forkynnelse er mer fysisk utmattende enn åtte timer med sleggearbeid. Så krevende er inspirert forkynnelse.
Vi takker dere alle i kveld. Hvis jeg har glemt noen... Å ja, organisten og pianisten... Det er så mange av dere. I dag har jeg bare snakket med forskjellige personer på ulike steder.

text:
And if I'm not mistaken, I believe I see a sweet, little old couple that usually calls me long distance when they're sick. And I don't believe God's failed one time. And I don't know if it is, but isn't that the Rev. and Mrs. Kidd sitting right there? Raise up your hand.
I want you all to see an old veteran and his wife, way in their seventies or eighties, still preaching the Gospel. Would you stand up just a minute? Brother and Sister Kidd. Let's say, "Praise the Lord" for God's goodness to them. That's very much appropriate. God bless them. God bless you, sister and brother. Way in the years of eighty and still preaching the Gospel. Oh, isn't that wonderful? I pray that God will bless them exceedingly abundantly.
And now, they were probably out preaching before I was born. And you see how honored I feel of having them. You know, usually when you get a little old, the general public seems to pass you by. But Brother and Sister Kidd, there's one who stood by Paul and Silas. He will never leave thee, nor forsake thee.
text_no:
Og hvis jeg ikke tar feil, tror jeg at jeg ser et søtt, lite gammelt par som vanligvis ringer meg langdistanse når de er syke. Og jeg tror ikke Gud har sviktet en eneste gang. Jeg vet ikke om det er dem, men er ikke det pastor og fru Kidd som sitter der? Løft opp hånden.
Jeg vil at dere alle skal se en gammel veteran og hans kone, godt i sytti- eller åttiårene, som fortsatt forkynner Evangeliet. Kan dere reise dere et øyeblikk? Bror og Søster Kidd. La oss si: "Pris Herren" for Guds godhet mot dem. Det er svært passende. Gud velsigne dem. Gud velsigne deg, søster og bror. Godt oppe i åttiårene og fortsatt forkynnende Evangeliet. Å, er ikke det vidunderlig? Jeg ber at Gud vil velsigne dem overmåte rikelig.
Og nå, de var trolig ute og forkynte før jeg ble født. Ser dere hvor beæret jeg føler meg over å ha dem her? Vanligvis, når man blir litt eldre, ser det ut til at allmennheten overser en. Men Bror og Søster Kidd, det er Én som sto ved Paulus og Silas. Han vil aldri forlate dere, eller svikte dere.

text:
I want to read tonight from the blessed Word. And then just for a short time, being hoarse… And we will pray for the sick.
And I want to thank you once more for something else: praying for me last night. The first time I ever got a prayer line through like that, in I don't know when, without visions, because I just went to that all the time. And how the testimonies has poured in today, it's been wonderful.
What you need is a great big tent sitting out here in about a four or five weeks' revival, so you can just stay right with it until it's over.
text_no:
I ønsker å lese fra det velsignede Ordet i kveld. Deretter vil vi, for en kort stund, be for de syke, på tross av at jeg er hes.
Jeg vil også takke dere en gang til for noe annet: for bønnene dere ba for meg i går kveld. Det var første gang på lenge at jeg kunne lede en bønnekø uten visjoner, da jeg har vært avhengig av det en god stund nå. Vitnesbyrdene som har strømmet inn i dag, har vært vidunderlige.
Det vi trenger, er et stort telt her ute for en fire til fem ukers vekkelse, slik at vi kan holde på helt til det er ferdig.

text:
I want to read from the book of Saint Luke tonight, from the 18th chapter of Saint Luke, and beginning at the 42nd verse.
And Jesus said unto him, Receive thy sight: thy faith hath saved thee.
And immediately he received his sight, and glorified him, glorifying God: and all the people, when they saw it, gave praise unto God.
And may the Lord add His blessings to the reading of His Word. And may the Almighty Jehovah ever bless.
Usually this is the night when more is done, than any other night in the meeting, because the great strain: It's the anticipation, the waiting, the expectancy. And that's usually what brings the Holy Spirit right down. Oh, how I pray with all my heart, that there will not be a feeble one among us in an hour from now. I pray not one sinner in the building… May every sinner be saved, every backslider come back to the house of God, is my humble, sincere prayer.
text_no:
I kveld vil jeg lese fra Evangeliet etter Lukas, kapittel 18, fra vers 42.
Jesus sa til ham: «Bli seende! Din tro har frelst deg.» 
Straks ble han seende, og han priste Gud. Hele folkemengden som så dette, ga Gud ære. 
Må Herren legge Sin velsignelse til lesningen av Ordet. Må Den Allmektige Jehova alltid velsigne oss. 
Vanligvis er dette kvelden hvor det skjer mer enn noen annen kveld i møtene, på grunn av den store spenningen: forventningen, ventingen, forventningen. Og det er vanligvis hva som får Den Hellige Ånd til å komme ned. Åh, hvor jeg ber med hele mitt hjerte om at det ikke skal finnes en eneste svak person blant oss om en time. Jeg ber om at ingen syndere skal forbli i bygningen... Måtte hver synder bli frelst og hver frafalne komme tilbake til Guds hus, er min ydmyke, oppriktige bønn.

text:
Jesus had just been speaking to the disciples about sacrilegious: how that there was a publican went to the temple to pray, and a self-righteous man stood there. And how he in all of his good deeds that he could quote to the Lord: "How I thank Thee that I'm not like this publican or like ordinary men, how I pay my tithes and fast," and so forth. But Jesus was teaching them that all those things were all right, and they had their place, but then those things are not the real things.
They're not the religious. They're religion, but they're not salvation. If we depend upon those things to merit for our salvation, we would go in by works and wouldn't need the Lord Jesus. But we have to solemnly depend upon Him, and I'm so glad. Then everyone can come. If religion only means to build a church, to sacrifice and to be sincere, God would be unjust to condemn Cain, because he done everything religious that Abel did. Everything that Abel did religious, Cain did the same. Both boys sincere; both boys made an altar; both boys made a sacrifice; both boys knelt down; both boys prayed; both boys worshipped.
So if worship, sincerity, belonging to church, and all those things are all God requires, then He would be unjust to condemn Cain, because Cain did the very same thing that Abel did. But all of it was works of his hands. And it's not by works; it's by the grace of God we are saved. Abel never worked; He just believed on Him that justifies the ungodly. And his sacrifice was received.
text_no:
Jesus hadde nettopp snakket med disiplene om hvordan en toller gikk til tempelet for å be, mens en selvrettferdig mann stod der og roste seg selv. Denne mannen fortalte Herren om alle sine gode gjerninger: "Jeg takker Deg for at jeg ikke er som denne tolleren eller vanlige mennesker, for jeg betaler tiende og faster," og så videre. Jesus lærte dem at selv om disse handlingene er gode og har sin plass, er de ikke de virkelige tingene. De er religiøse gjerninger, men de er ikke frelse. Hvis vi baserer vår frelse på slike gjerninger, ville vi gå inn ved arbeider, og da ville vi ikke trenge Herren Jesus. Vi må avhengig av Ham alene, og det er jeg så glad for. Da kan alle komme.
Hvis religion bare betydde å bygge en menighet, ofre og være oppriktig, ville Gud være urettferdig med å fordømme Kain, for han gjorde alt religiøst som Abel gjorde. Abel og Kain gjorde samme religiøse handlinger. Begge var oppriktige, begge bygde et alter, begge ofret, begge knelte, begge ba, og begge tilbad. Så hvis tilbedelse, oppriktighet og medlemskap i menigheten er alt Gud krever, ville Han være urettferdig med å fordømme Kain, for Kain gjorde det samme som Abel gjorde. Men alt dette var arbeider av hans hender. Det er ikke ved arbeider; vi er frelst av Guds nåde. Abel jobbet ikke; Han bare trodde på Ham som rettferdiggjør de ugudelige. Og hans offer ble tatt imot.

text:
Our scene tonight starts at the gate of Jericho. Jericho was one of the lowest cities of the world, way down in the valley of the Jordan. And it's a pathetic scene as we look at it. There is a blind man by the name of Bartimaeus. And he's sitting by the wall of Jericho.
And it was pretty hard on the beggars of those days, because there were many of them. There were lepers; there were cripples, lame, halt, blind, all types of beggars. And as a man who was a little more blessed that could spare a coin now and then, when he left his home at morning, the first beggar he met he, perhaps, give him the coin, and maybe could not afford anymore through the day. So it was the custom that beggars would stay near the gate, that when the traveler, or the businessman, coming to his business in the city would drop the coin to them, that they might have a livelihood.
text_no:
Vår scene i kveld begynner ved porten til Jeriko. Jeriko var en av verdens lavest beliggende byer, langt nede i Jordandalen. Det er et patetisk syn vi ser på. Der sitter en blind mann ved navn Bartimeus ved muren til Jeriko.
For tiggerne i de dager var det ganske vanskelig, for det var mange av dem. Det var spedalske, krøplinger, lamme, halte, blinde – alle slags tiggere. En mann som var litt mer velsignet og kunne avse en mynt nå og da, ga kanskje den første tiggeren han møtte om morgenen en mynt, men kunne kanskje ikke avse flere i løpet av dagen. Det var derfor vanlig at tiggerne oppholdt seg nær porten, slik at når reisende eller forretningsfolk kom inn til byen, kunne de gi en mynt til tiggerne, som på den måten kunne få sitt levebrød.

text:
And perhaps, this man being blind for many years, if there had've been an operation could've been performed on his eyes, he could not afford it, for he was a beggar. But very much in doubt whether physicians of that day could help him, or the physicians of the day now. Blindness is a horrible thing; an eye is a valuable thing.
And so while he was sitting there… Let's just take the little picture now and go down there with blind Bartimaeus, so we can get the real kernel of what we wish to talk on for the next fifteen, twenty minutes.
text_no:
Denne mannen hadde kanskje vært blind i mange år, og hvis det hadde vært mulig å operere øynene hans, kunne han ikke ha råd til det, for han var en tigger. Men det er tvilsomt om datidens leger kunne ha hjulpet ham, eller dagens leger for den saks skyld. Blindhet er en forferdelig tilstand; et øye er en verdifull ting.
Så mens han satt der... La oss nå fokusere på situasjonen til blinde Bartimeus, slik at vi kan få fram kjernen i det vi ønsker å snakke om de neste femten til tjue minuttene.

text:
Blind Bartimaeus, sitting by the gate, perhaps, in the sunshine of the direct rays of the Palestinian sun shining on him, by the wall… Let's think that he was studying, meditating.
You know, the way to find God is to meditate upon God, think of God. David spoke of it. How he would bind the laws on his bedposts, and how he would meditate therein day and night.
If you notice it, as the disciples on the road to Emmaus, that while they were speaking to each other, concerning the Lord Jesus, that He stepped right out of the bush and walked with them.
If our minds would be more taken up on God and on the things pertaining to God, it would be so much easier to live the victorious life in Christ. He would be with us.
text_no:
Blind Bartimaeus satt ved porten, kanskje i solskinnet fra den palestinske solen som strålte på ham, ved muren. La oss tenke oss at han studerte og mediterte.
Vet du, veien til å finne Gud er å meditere over Gud, å tenke på Gud. David snakket om det. Hvordan han ville binde lovene ved sine sengeposter og meditert over dem dag og natt.
Legg merke til det, som med disiplene på veien til Emmaus, at mens de snakket med hverandre om Herren Jesus, steg Han rett ut fra busken og gikk med dem.
Hvis våre tanker var mer opptatt av Gud og tingene som angår Gud, ville det være mye lettere å leve et seirende liv i Kristus. Han ville være med oss.

text:
You notice Eliezer while he was thinking, meditating to God, the beautiful Rebekah come out to water his camels just in answer to what he was asking God.
And as blind Bartimaeus sat at the gate… He was a beggar, ragged, sitting with his little rags wrapped around him, probably a middle-aged man. We're kind of taught that he had a family, a wife, a little girl. And as he sat there in the sunshine, I can just imagine his mind drifting back to another day, a day that when things was better for him, when he was a little boy as he went over the Judean hills.
And I can think that maybe, perhaps, one of his favorite times was when his … after a midday meal was over, as a little brown-eyed Jewish boy frolics along in the soft wind and skips through the fields. He would come in at a certain hour to his mama, a beautiful young Jewess. And as his favorite thing was, before he taken his afternoon nap, was to hear her tell stories of the Bible.
text_no:
Mens Eliezer tenkte og mediterte til Gud, så han den vakre Rebekka komme for å gi kamelene vann, akkurat som svar på det han ba Gud om.
Blind Bartimeus satt ved porten. Han var en tigger, fillete og sannsynligvis en mann i middelalderen. Vi tror han hadde en familie, en kone og en liten jente. Mens han satt der i solskinnet, kan jeg se for meg at tankene hans vandret tilbake til en annen tid, en tid da livet var bedre for ham, da han var en liten gutt som løp over de judeiske åsene.
Jeg kan tenke meg at kanskje ett av hans favorittøyeblikk var etter middag, da han som en liten bruneøyd jødisk gutt lekte i den milde vinden og hoppet gjennom åkrene. Han kom inn til sin mor på en bestemt time, en vakker ung jødisk kvinne. Hans favorittøvelse før ettermiddagsluren var å høre henne fortelle historier fra Bibelen.

text:
That seems to be so interesting to any little boy that's got good character in them: the Bible stories. It's just too bad that mothers today don't take more time to talk to their children on those things. I'm sure we wouldn't have so much juvenile delinquency if this was did.
And as his little bright eyes would look up into mama's face… And how he would enjoy the different stories that she would read from the Bible to him. (The Old Testament of course, 'cause that's all they had.)
text_no:
Bibelfortellinger er alltid så interessante for enhver gutt med god karakter. Det er synd at mødre i dag ikke tar seg tid til å snakke med barna sine om disse tingene. Jeg er sikker på at vi ville hatt mindre ungdomskriminalitet hvis dette ble gjort.
Når hans små, lyse øyne så opp på mammas ansikt, hvordan han ville nyte de forskjellige fortellingene hun leste for ham fra Bibelen. (Det Gamle Testamentet, selvsagt, for det var alt de hadde.)

text:
And perhaps one of the leading stories that he liked so well, was the one of the great mighty prophet Elijah, how that Elijah in his day, how that he went up to Mount Carmel, and there was a Shunammite woman, a Gentile. She thought he was a great prophet, and she was a rich woman, and she could help this prophet; so she built herself a little house for him, a little room, little chamber. And she placed in there a bed, a little stool, and some water for his feet, and a pitcher to drink from, to show kindness.
And so one day when he found all this kindness, he sent Gehazi his servant, to pick her up, to bring her in to where he was. And he said, "Could I do a favor for you? You've been so kind to me to divide to me the portion of your living, and to help me to have a little place to rest. And you did this because that you had respect unto my God. Could I speak to the king for you, or to the chief captain?"
She said, "No, I dwell among my people. Everything's all right."
text_no:
En av historiene han likte best var fortellingen om den store, mektige profeten Elia. I hans dager dro Elia opp til Karmelfjellet, hvor det bodde en sunamittisk kvinne, en hedning. Hun mente Elia var en stor profet, og som en rik kvinne ønsket hun å hjelpe ham. Derfor bygde hun et lite hus for ham, et lite rom, komplett med en seng, en krakk, vann til føttene og en krukke til drikke, for å vise vennlighet.
En dag, da Elia oppdaget all denne gjestfriheten, sendte han sin tjener Gehazi for å hente henne. Elia spurte, "Kan jeg gjøre deg en tjeneste? Du har vært så vennlig og delt av din velstand, og hjulpet meg med et sted å hvile. Dette gjorde du av respekt for min Gud. Skal jeg tale til kongen eller øverste hærfører for deg?"
Hun svarte, "Nei, jeg bor blant mitt folk. Alt er bra."

text:
And Gehazi said to him, "She doesn't have any children." And perhaps, Elijah in his prayer saw what the Lord was going to do. And he said, "Go tell her that about this time next year, according to life, she is going to embrace a son."
And the little boy was born in the home, when her husband was old. And he was a lovely little lad. How little Bartimaeus' eyes would glisten and just sparkle when he could hear of this little boy born. And how mommy would baby him and play with him, and then one day it came to pass when he was about the age of Bartimaeus then, was out in the field with his papa, and it must have been a sunstroke. It was about near noon, eleven o'clock, and the little boy begin to cry, "My head, my head." And the father, being busy in the harvest, sent a servant, took the little lad home, and the mother laid him on her lap, and he died. Oh, what a tragedy.
text_no:
Gehazi sa til ham: "Hun har ingen barn." Og kanskje så Elia i sin bønn hva Herren skulle gjøre. Han sa: "Gå og fortell henne at om omtrent et år, etter livets gang, skal hun omfavne en sønn."
Gutten ble født i hjemmet, selv om ektemannen hennes var gammel. Han var en herlig liten gutt. Baritmeus' øyne ville glitre når han hørte om denne gutten. Moren skjemte bort og lekte med ham. En dag, da gutten var på alder med Bartimeus, var han ute i åkeren med sin far. Det må ha vært et solstikk, rundt klokken elleve. Gutten begynte å klage: "Hodet mitt, hodet mitt." Faren, som var opptatt med innhøstingen, sendte en tjener til å ta gutten hjem. Moren tok ham på fanget sitt, og han døde. For en tragedie.

text:
But to this Shunammite woman there was no defeat. She had something in her heart. She took the little dead form of the boy and laid him upon the bed of Elijah. What a place to put him. Just right. Take him, and might have laid him in his own little crib upon their bed, but took him around the house to the little prophet's chamber, and opened the door, and laid him where the servant of God had laid. What a wise woman! And she called her husband, and she said, "I must go find the man of God." I like that.
Oh he said, "It's neither new moon, nor Sabbath."
But she said, "It'll be all right." Something in her heart. "Everything will be well."
So he saddled a mule, and he told the servant---she did---said, "Ride, and don't stop till I call you to." And as they neared the mount where the prophet living in his cave, he walked out beside of the cave, and he looked and he said to the servant, "Here comes that Shunammite. Go and meet her. She seems like she's in a rush. Oh, they're just whipping the little mule, and he's a going. Go meet her and say unto her, 'Is all well with thee? Is all well with thy husband? Is all well with the baby?'"
text_no:
For denne kvinnen fra Sunem var det ingen nederlag. Hun hadde noe i sitt hjerte. Hun tok den lille døde kroppen til gutten og la ham på Elisas seng. For et sted å legge ham. Helt riktig. Hun kunne ha lagt ham i hans egen lille krybbe eller i deres seng, men hun bar ham gjennom huset til profetens rom, åpnet døren og la ham der Guds tjener hadde sovet. For en klok kvinne! Hun kalte på sin mann og sa, "Jeg må finne Guds mann." Det liker jeg.
Han svarte, "Det er verken nymåne eller sabbat."
Men hun sa, "Det går bra." Noe i hennes hjerte fortalte henne, "Alt vil bli bra."
Så salte hun et esel og sa til tjeneren, "Ritt, og ikke stopp før jeg sier fra." Da de nærmet seg fjellet hvor profeten bodde i sin hule, kom han ut og så dem. Han sa til tjeneren, "Her kommer kvinnen fra Sunem. Gå og møt henne. Hun virker å ha hastverk. Se hvordan de pisker det lille eselet, og det går raskt. Gå og spør henne, 'Står det bra til med deg? Står det bra til med din mann? Står det bra til med barnet?'"

text:
And his servant ran and met her. And she said, "All is well." ---the baby dead, her husband frantic---said, "All is well." Why? She knew God was in His servant, the prophet. She knew she could hear from God, and she wanted to know why that her baby had been taken. So she runs up close, and Elijah looked at her again, and he said, "Her heart is full of grief, and God has hid it from me."
You know, there's a wonderful thing. God don't tell His prophets everything. He just tells them what He wants them to know, not everything. They can do nothing in themselves. Just what God shows them, that's what they do.
Could you imagine Isaac sitting blind and blessing Jacob instead of Esau? Could you imagine Jacob holding Joseph's coat for forty years, bloody, thinking that an animal killed it, and being a prophet?
Proves God just reveals to His prophets that what He wants them to know, nothing else. Prophets are not infallible people. Prophets are not angels. They are men.
text_no:
Hans tjener løp og møtte henne. Hun sa: "Alt står bra til." ---barnet var dødt, mannen hennes i panikk---men hun sa fortsatt: "Alt står bra til." Hvorfor? Hun visste at Gud var i Sin tjener, profeten. Hun visste at hun kunne høre fra Gud, og hun ville forstå hvorfor barnet hadde blitt tatt fra henne. Så hun løper frem, og Elisa ser på henne igjen og sier: "Hennes hjerte er fullt av sorg, og Gud har skjult det for meg."
Det er en vidunderlig ting: Gud forteller ikke Sine profeter alt. Han forteller dem bare det Han vil at de skal vite, ikke alt. De kan ikke gjøre noe av seg selv. Bare det Gud viser dem, er det de gjør.
Kan du forestille deg Isak, blind, som velsigner Jakob i stedet for Esau? Kan du forestille deg Jakob som i førti år holder Josefs blodige kappe, og tror at et dyr har drept ham, til tross for at han var en profet?
Dette beviser at Gud bare åpenbarer til Sine profeter det Han vil at de skal vite, og ikke noe annet. Profeter er ikke ufeilbarlige mennesker. Profeter er ikke engler. De er menn.

text:
In Saint James the 5th chapter it said Elijah was a man subject to like passions as we are. He had his ups-and-downs. But he prayed earnestly that it might not rain. Then he goes to say, "The effectual, fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much."
And God had not revealed this to Elijah. And she ran up to his feet and fell down and made known everything that had happened. Now, Elijah knew that God dwelt in him, so he said to Gehazi, "Take my staff and go lay it on the child. Now, don't stop along the road for a little chat here and there. If any man salutes you, don't salute him back."
That's the trouble with the church today with this message, they're stopping, chatting along the road, little gatherings, and social parties, and card parties, and picnics, and sewing circles, and so forth. Too much of the world has entered into the [unclear words] of the blessed message. Oh, I hope you see this.
text_no:
I Jakobs brev, kapittel 5, står det at Elia var et menneske med samme natur som oss. Han hadde sine opp- og nedturer, men bad inderlig om at det ikke måtte regne. Videre står det: "En rettferdig manns bønn har stor kraft og virkning."
Gud hadde ikke åpenbart dette for Elia. Hun løp til hans føtter, falt ned og fortalte alt som hadde skjedd. Elia visste at Gud bodde i ham, så han sa til Gehazi: "Ta min stav og legg den på barnet. Ikke stans langs veien for småsnakk. Hvis noen hilser på deg, ikke hils tilbake."
Dette er problemet med menigheten i dag; de stopper opp, prater langs veien, holder små sammenkomster, sosiale fester, kortspill, piknik, og syklubber. For mye av verden har sneket seg inn i den velsignede budskapen. Å, jeg håper dere forstår dette.

text:
Notice. "If any man salute you, salute him not back, but go straight to the boy." That's what we need: Straight to the Word, none of that hanging around to this, that, or other, but right to the point. Notice. Then what happened?
But you know, I think that's where Paul got taking handkerchiefs and aprons from his body. Elijah knew that everything he touched was blessed, because God was in him. That's what the woman that touched the hem of the garment of Jesus. She knew God was in Him.
But you know, the woman's faith wasn't in the staff; it was in the prophet. So she said, "As the Lord liveth, I will not leave you. I'm just going to hang around." She had something in her heart, and she was staying with it.
That's the thing. If God has answered a faith in your heart that He's going to give you the Holy Spirit, stay right with it. If He puts something in your heart during the meeting, that He's going to heal you, stay right with it. Don't let nothing move it. Call everything that's happened contrary to that, call it wrong. Cast it aside and look to the promise.
text_no:
Legg merke til: "Hvis noen hilser på dere, så hils ikke tilbake, men gå rett til gutten." Det er dette vi trenger: Rett til Ordet, ingen omveier eller nøling, men rett på sak. Hva skjedde så?
Jeg tror Paulus hentet inspirasjon fra dette, når han brukte lommetørklær og forklær fra sitt eget legeme. Elia visste at alt han berørte, ble velsignet, fordi Gud var i ham. Slik var det også med kvinnen som rørte ved kanten av Jesu kappe. Hun visste at Gud var i Ham.
Men merk, kvinnens tro var ikke i staven; den var i profeten. Derfor sa hun: "Så sant Herren lever, jeg vil ikke forlate deg. Jeg blir her." Hun hadde noe i sitt hjerte, og hun holdt fast ved det.
Det er kjernen. Hvis Gud har gitt deg tro i ditt hjerte om at Han vil gi deg Den Hellige Ånd, hold fast ved det. Hvis Han under møtet har gitt deg en overbevisning om at du skal bli helbredet, hold fast ved det. La ingenting rikke deg. Alt som skjer i strid med dette, skal du avvise. Kast det bort og fest blikket på løftet.

text:
She said, "I'm going to stay right with you." And the prophet got tired of perhaps having her hanging around. So he goes and gets his mantle, and throws it over him, and girds up his loins, and here he went, the woman following him. Gehazi went ahead, and went into the dead baby, laid his stick upon the baby, but nothing happened. Why? The mother didn't believe it. It would've happened if the mother would've believed it. Maybe she didn't know about the stick, but she did know about the prophet. So she…
Then what taken place? Here comes the prophet up with the mother, her eyes right on him; and look what a condition it is. The neighbors was screaming and wailing. And a heartbroken father screaming out in the street, "Oh, my boy, my boy," all tore up. The prophet just goes to his own little chamber and closes the door behind him. Looked laying there on the bed, there laid a little stiff, cold form of a baby, dead several hours. And the prophet walked back-and-forth, to-and-fro, up-and-down in the room. I just love that: just walking to-and-fro, waiting on the Lord. "He that waits upon the Lord shall renew his strength; he will mount up like an eagle; he will run and not be weary; he will walk and not faint."
text_no:
Hun sa: "Jeg vil bli her med deg." Og profeten ble kanskje lei av at hun stadig hang rundt ham. Så han tar kappen sin, kaster den over seg, binder opp kjortelen og går av sted, med kvinnen følgende etter. Gehazi gikk foran, kom til den døde babyen og la staven sin på barnet, men ingenting skjedde. Hvorfor? Moren trodde ikke på det. Det ville ha skjedd hvis moren hadde trodd. Kanskje visste hun ikke om staven, men hun kjente til profeten.
Hva skjedde så? Profeten kom opp sammen med moren, hennes øyne festet på ham; og se hvilken tilstand det var. Naboene skrek og klaget. En sønderknust far ropte ute på gaten: "Åh, gutten min, gutten min," helt fortvilet. Profeten gikk bare til sitt eget lille kammer og lukket døren bak seg. På sengen lå den stive, kalde kroppen av en baby, død i flere timer. Profeten gikk frem og tilbake, opp og ned i rommet. Jeg elsker det: bare gå frem og tilbake, vente på Herren. "De som venter på Herren skal fornye sin kraft; de skal løfte seg med vinger som ørner; de skal løpe og ikke bli trette; de skal gå og ikke besvime."

text:
And he walked to-and-fro, waiting on the Lord. After a bit something must have struck the prophet, when he knowed the Holy Spirit was on his body. He ran to the child, put his hands on its hands, put his nose against its nose, and the baby sneezed seven times and come to life. Outside they went. What a happy home!
And how little Bartimaeus must've loved that story. And as he sat there renewing it in his mind, and how the great conqueror Joshua and many of the other stories; but alas, he shakes himself. The sun has moved over; a shade has come over him. He goes along with his blind eyes and sits down again. Somehow he never got a coin maybe. That morning. What's he going to tell his little girl when he goes home? He perhaps maybe wanted to see if she could get a dress. His poor wife maybe hadn't had one in a long time. And he was ragged, and he couldn't even get soup for their meal.
And now, maybe that they're hungry this morning, and now what can he do? Nothing to eat, and ragged and sitting there. But there's one thing he had, was memories of a day gone by.
text_no:
Han gikk frem og tilbake, ventende på Herren. Etter en stund må noe ha slått ned i profeten da han kjente at Den Hellige Ånd kom over ham. Han løp til barnet, la hendene sine på dets hender, satte nesen mot nesen, og barnet nøs syv ganger og kom til live. Ute gikk de. For et lykkelig hjem!
Hvor mye må ikke Bartimeus ha elsket den fortellingen. Der han satt og gjenopplevde den i sitt sinn, med minner om den store seierherren Josva og mange andre historier; men dessverre ristet han seg selv ut av dagdrømmen. Solen hadde beveget seg, og en skygge hadde lagt seg over ham. Med sine blinde øyne vandret han videre og satte seg ned igjen. Kanskje han ikke hadde fått en mynt den morgenen. Hva skulle han fortelle sin lille datter når han kom hjem? Han ønsket kanskje å se om han kunne skaffe henne en kjole. Hans stakkars kone hadde kanskje ikke hatt en på lenge. Han var fillete og kunne knapt få tak i suppe til måltidet deres.
Kanskje var de sultne denne morgenen, og hva kunne han gjøre? Ingenting å spise, fillete og sittende der. Men det var én ting han hadde: minner fra en svunnen tid.

text:
And then he remembered that not thirty feet from where he was sitting the great prophet Elijah and Elisha walked down that road together, arm in arm.
Oh, I can imagine to hear him within his heart, "Oh, if I would've only been sitting here then. I would've raised and said, 'Oh, prophet of God, you great mighty prophet, speak to Jehovah for me.' But, oh, the days of miracles is past, so my priest tells me. That was Jehovah in a day gone by. But think of it. One day right down where I'm sitting within thirty feet went those two mighty prophets, that walked right down to that Jordan and smote the Jordan and opened it up."
Surely in his heart he was saying, "That Jehovah didn't die." And of the great coming out of the children of Israel, when the oldest son was killed because there was no blood on the lintel of the door, would be another favorite story for him. And also of just a few hundred yards from where he was sitting, the great mighty Joshua delivering the people into the promised land, walked down to the Jordan, taking the ark to where that God in the front of them, and it opened up. And His people walked across on dry land.
text_no:
Han husket at ikke mer enn ti meter fra der han satt, hadde de store profetene Elia og Elisja gått sammen, arm i arm. Jeg kan levende forestille meg hvordan han følte: "Å, om jeg bare hadde sittet her da. Jeg ville ha reist meg og sagt: 'Å, Guds profet, du store og mektige profet, tal til Jehova for meg.' Men, åh, min prest sier at miraklenes tid er forbi. Det var Jehova i en svunnen tid. Men tenk på det. En dag, rett her hvor jeg sitter, gikk de to mektige profetene, som gikk ned til Jordan, slo på vannet og åpnet det."
Sikkerlig i sitt hjerte sa han, "Den Jehova døde ikke." En annen favorittfortelling for ham ville være om Israels barns store utgang, da den eldste sønnen ble drept fordi det ikke var blod på dørstolpen. Og bare noen få hundre meter fra der han satt, hadde den store og mektige Josva ført folket inn i det lovede land. Han gikk ned til Jordan, bar Arken med Gud foran dem, og åpnet vannet. Hans folk gikk over på tørr grunn.

text:
"Oh," in his heart, "we are still the people of God. We are circumcised according to the precepts of the Testament, but they tell me that Jehovah doesn't do those things anymore." You know, times hasn't changed too much. And I say that with respect.
And then he thought again, not over two hundred yards from where he was sitting then, one day when the mighty Joshua, the great prince, was walking out viewing the walls of Jericho, before the great battle, he saw a man standing with a drawn sword.
He had oft heard his mother tell that story, read it to him out of the Bible. And as he stood with his sword drawn, Joshua might've thought, "Where did He come from?" He just appeared, and He was a man with His sword drawed. And Joshua drew his sword and went to meet Him. And as Joshua went to meet Him, he said, "Are You for us? Are You for the enemy?"
And the man spoke back to this Joshua and said, "No. I am the Captain of the host of the Lord," just two hundred yards from where he was sitting. Little did he know that that same Captain of the Lord's host was not two hundred yards from him right then.
text_no:
"Oh," tenkte han i sitt hjerte, "vi er fortsatt Guds folk. Vi er omskåret i henhold til Testamentets forskrifter, men de sier at Jehova ikke gjør disse tingene lenger." Du vet, tider har ikke forandret seg så mye. Og jeg sier det med respekt.
Så tenkte han igjen, ikke mer enn to hundre meter fra hvor han satt, en dag da den mektige Josva, den store prinsen, gikk rundt for å betrakte Jerikos murer før den store slaget, og så en mann stående med et dratt sverd.
Han hadde ofte hørt moren fortelle den historien, lese den for ham fra Bibelen. Og da han sto med sitt sverd i hånden, kan Josva ha tenkt: "Hvor kom Han fra?" Han bare dukket opp, og Han var en mann med sitt sverd dratt. Josva trakk sitt sverd og gikk for å møte Ham. Og da Josva gikk for å møte Ham, sa han: "Er Du for oss? Eller er Du for fienden?"
Og mannen svarte denne Josva og sa: "Nei. Jeg er Hærføreren for Herrens hær," ikke mer enn to hundre meter fra hvor han satt. Lite visste han at den samme Hærføreren for Herrens hær var like i nærheten av ham akkurat da.

text:
And as He begin to come into Jerusalem … or, to Jericho, there begin to be clatter everywhere, people running, after a while noises, people talking, different [unclear words] subjects, some one way, some another. He listened. He was blind. And as he listened. "What is this?"
And after while there come a scream of criticism, people hollering, "Here comes that fanatic. Here comes that man that speaks against our priests and our church. Let's not let him enter the city." Others come, and above all he heard the great roar of his priest which said, "Hey, you Galilean, you who can raise a dead man, we've got a whole graveyard full of them up here on the hill. Come up and raise some of them."
That was the kind of mockery He got. That's the kind He gets today. But Jesus never heard them. Perhaps, rotten cabbage, or overripe eggs thrown at Him, and making fun of Him, and said, "You who has done all these miracles, let me see You do something. Ha-ha. You're soothsayers. There's nothing to this fanatic. He doesn't join up with our schools. And there's nothing to Him to begin with."
text_no:
Da Han begynte å nærme seg Jeriko, brøt det ut liv og røre overalt. Folk løp rundt, det var støy og forskjellige samtaleemner; noen snakket om én ting, andre om noe annet. Han lyttet. Han var blind. Da han lyttet, spurte han: "Hva er dette?"
Etter en stund hørte han kritiske rop, folk som skrek: "Her kommer den fanatikeren. Her kommer mannen som taler imot våre prester og vår menighet. La oss ikke slippe ham inn i byen." Andre kom, og over alt hørte han prestens høye rop: "Hei, du galileer, du som kan vekke de døde, vi har en hel gravlund full av døde her på høyden. Kom opp og vekk noen av dem."
Slik spott ble Han møtt med. Slik spott møter Han også i dag. Men Jesus hørte dem aldri. Kanskje ble råtten kål eller overmodne egg kastet på Ham, og de gjorde narr av Ham og sa: "Du som har gjort alle disse miraklene, la meg se Deg gjøre noe nå. Ha-ha. Du er en spåmann. Det er ingenting ved denne fanatikeren. Han er ikke med på vår skole. Det er ingenting i Ham i det hele tatt."

text:
And Bartimaeus begin to wonder, "Who is this such a person, wonder who this could be?" Then he hears another voice, "Hosanna, Hosanna! The prophet of Galilee comes." What was he saying? "Hosanna! The great prophet Jesus comes to the city. Make way for Him! Give to Him songs! Give to Him praise!"
And Bartimaeus said, "Who passes?" No one pays any attention to this poor old beggar, too insignificant to be looked at when they came everywhere. But after while a kind little lady stooped down. And he said, "Madam, who is passing by?"
She said, "Oh, blind man, do you… Have you not heard that Jesus of Nazareth is the Messiah of God?"
"Oh," he says, "I heard my mama tell me that someday the Messiah would come, but surely the priests wouldn't be talking about Him like that, and saying all those things."
But she said, "It is true. This Man is the Messiah."
"Oh, you mean that the Son of David is coming through and they're treating Him that way?"
"Yes, that's Him."
text_no:
Bartimeus begynner å undre, "Hvem er denne personen, hvem kan det være?" Så hører han en annen stemme, "Hosanna, Hosanna! Profeten fra Galilea kommer." Hva var det han sa? "Hosanna! Den store profeten Jesus kommer til byen. Gjør plass for Ham! Gi Ham lovsanger! Gi Ham pris!"
Bartimeus spør, "Hvem passerer?" Ingen bryr seg om denne fattige, gamle tiggeren; han er for ubetydelig til å bli lagt merke til midt i folkemengden. Men etter en stund bøyer en vennlig liten dame seg ned. Han spør, "Frue, hvem er det som passerer?"
Hun svarer, "Å, blinde mann, har du ikke hørt at Jesus fra Nasaret er Guds Messias?"
"Å," sier han, "jeg hørte min mor fortelle at en dag ville Messias komme, men prestene ville vel ikke snakke om Ham på den måten."
Men hun sa, "Det er sant. Denne Mannen er Messias."
"Å, du mener at Davids Sønn kommer gjennom her, og de behandler Ham slik?"
"Ja, det er Ham."

text:
Then all of his dreams, all of his fond hopes jumped into action; he sprang to his feet, and screamed, "Oh, Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me!"
And the people said, "Oh, shut up; sit down: you know that there's no such a thing as any help for you, blind Bartimaeus. The days are past for those things. That fellow is just a fanatic."
Down in his heart where he'd been dreaming… "But mother read to me out of the Bible that Moses said, 'The Lord your God shall rise up a prophet likened unto me.'" How that the prophet Isaiah said the lame would leap like an hart, and how the blind would see when this Prophet came. And faith took a hold, and he cried the much more, "Thou Son of David, have mercy on me!"
text_no:
Da sprang alle hans drømmer og håp til live. Han reiste seg og ropte: "Å, Jesus, Du Davids Sønn, forbarm Deg over meg!"
Folket svarte: "Å, vær stille; sett deg ned. Du vet at det ikke er noen hjelp for deg, blinde Bartimeus. Slike ting skjer ikke lenger. Den mannen er bare en fanatiker."
Men dypere inne i hjertet hvor han hadde drømt, mintes han: "Men mor leste for meg fra Bibelen at Moses sa, 'Herren deres Gud skal oppreise en profet lik meg.'" Og hvordan profeten Jesaja sa at de lamme skulle hoppe som hjort, og de blinde skulle se når denne Profeten kom. Troen grep fatt, og han ropte enda høyere: "Du Davids Sønn, forbarm Deg over meg!"

text:
Now, He could not hear the literal voice of the blind man with all that there screaming, and hollering, and saying the things, and mocking, and scoffing, and praises together. He would not hear physically, because He was too far from him.
And Jesus in this certain time, He knew that just a few days hence He was going … He was on His road down to Jerusalem. And there He must be mocked, and spit on, and made fun of, and beaten, and His back scored with a cat-o-nine-tail, or Roman lash, and there had to go to the cross. The great nails was in His mind, the thorns on His head, the spear in His heart, the agony of Gethsemane. All that was loaded on His precious heart. And I can see Him undisturbed by the criticism.
text_no:
Nå kunne Han ikke høre den blinde mannens bokstavelige stemme med all den skrikingen, ropingen, håningen og hylingen, alt sammen. Han kunne ikke fysisk høre, for Han var for langt borte fra ham.
Jesus visste på dette tidspunktet at om noen få dager skulle Han dra til Jerusalem. Der ville Han bli hånet, spyttet på, gjort narr av, slått, og Hans rygg ville bli skamfert av en romersk pisk. Han måtte gå til korset. De store spikrene, tornene på Hodet, spydet i Hjertet og lidelsene i Getsemane – alt dette veide tungt på Hans verdifulle Hjerte. Likevel forble Han uberørt av kritikken.

text:
You know, a big man can be criticized and he pays no attention. But it's the little one that speaks the loudest. So He wasn't paying attention when they were criticizing, making fun of Him; He had one thing: that was doing the will of His Father. With His heart full, to know that He came to His own, and His own received Him not, and knowing that He was to die for their very sins. He had His face set towards Jerusalem's Calvary. All that on His heart, He never noticed the flying missiles, and the criticisms, and so forth. He was walking towards Calvary.
So He could not have heard that man's voice. If you was ever at Jericho and watched the distance to the road … and all the crowd, but something happened. I can imagine as He passed by, and the crowd still hollering, the lady said, "Oh, sir. Cry out again."
He said, "Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me! Oh, He couldn't hear me, so I can see. O Father God, I believe with all my heart that this One who's persecuted and made fun of, is Your Son. I believe He is the Son of David."
text_no:
En stor mann kan bli kritisert uten å bry seg. Men det er den lille som roper høyest. Derfor brydde Han seg ikke når de kritiserte og gjorde narr av Ham; Han hadde ett fokus: å gjøre Faderens vilje. Med hjertet fylt av vissheten om at Han kom til sine egne, men de tok ikke imot Ham, og vel vitende om at Han skulle dø for deres synder, hadde Han satt blikket mot Golgata i Jerusalem. Med alt dette på hjertet, la Han ikke merke til de flyvende missilene og kritikken. Han gikk mot Golgata.
Så Han kunne ikke ha hørt den mannens stemme. Hvis du noen gang har vært i Jeriko og sett veien og menneskemengden, ville du forstått. Men noe skjedde. Jeg kan forestille meg at da Han gikk forbi og mengden fortsatt ropte, sa damen: "Å, sir. Rop igjen."
Han sa: "Jesus, Du Davids Sønn, ha miskunn med meg! Å, Han kunne ikke høre meg, så jeg kan se. O Far Gud, jeg tror av hele mitt hjerte at denne som er forfulgt og gjort narr av, er Din Sønn. Jeg tror Han er Davids Sønn."

text:
And something happened. That blind beggar's faith touched the Lord Jesus. Oh, the insignificant one that they tried to make him keep still, but his faith touched the Lord Jesus, like the woman that touched His garments. He turned, looking at him, and said, "Bring him here."
And when he got there, He said, "What is it that I could do for you?"
Said, "Lord, that I might receive my sight."
He said, "Thy faith has saved thee. If your faith was strong enough in God to call the virtue to leave Me, to call back to you, your faith is sufficient for your healing."
And as He turned and goes on, blind Bartimaeus standing there, and to all the black world before him became light. He could see his hand. And down the road he went rejoicing, praising God for his sight.
text_no:
Noe skjedde. Den blinde tiggerens tro rørte ved Herren Jesus. Å, den ubetydelige som de prøvde å få til å tie, men hans tro rørte ved Herren Jesus, som kvinnen som rørte ved Hans klær. Han snudde seg, så på ham, og sa: "Bring ham hit."
Da han kom frem, spurte Herren: "Hva vil du at Jeg skal gjøre for deg?"
Han svarte: "Herre, at jeg må få mitt syn."
Jesus sa: "Din tro har frelst deg. Hvis din tro på Gud var sterk nok til å få kraft til å forlate Meg og vende tilbake til deg, så er din tro tilstrekkelig for din helbredelse."
Da Jesus snudde seg og fortsatte, ble hele Bartimeus' mørke verden lys. Han kunne se hånden sin. Og nedover veien gikk han, jublende og prisende Gud for sitt syn.

text:
Not long ago I was reading a little story of him. It said that his wife and his baby, little girl, just about ten years old… He'd never seen her. He had been blind for years. Let's say he might have been a Christian; I do not know. But they said that in those days, while blind Bartimaeus begged at the gate, he had two little turtledoves that done little tumbles over each other. They entertained [unclear words] the tourists so he could get coins for this, something.
If you've ever been to India or so forth, you see the beggars have to have some attraction, or they won't attract the tourists.
So these little doves would tumble over each other. And one night, it was said, that his little girl got sick. And blind Bartimaeus made his way along the wall, and he prayed for the little girl, and said, "If You will let my little girl get well, God, tomorrow I will sacrifice one of my turtledoves to You." And the little girl got well, so he did.
text_no:
For ikke lenge siden leste jeg en liten historie om ham. Det ble sagt at hans kone og lille datter, som var omtrent ti år gammel, var med ham. Han hadde aldri sett henne, for han hadde vært blind i flere år. La oss anta at han kan ha vært en kristen; det vet jeg ikke. De sa at i de dager, mens blinde Bartimeus tigget ved porten, hadde han to små turtelduer som gjorde små ruller over hverandre. De underholdt turistene slik at han kunne få noen mynter for dette.
Hvis du noen gang har vært i India eller lignende steder, vil du se at tiggerne må ha noe som tiltrekker seg turister, ellers vil de ikke få oppmerksomhet.
Så disse små duene rullet over hverandre. En natt, ble det sagt, ble hans lille datter syk. Blind Bartimeus famlet seg langs veggen og ba for henne, og sa: "Hvis Du lar min lille jente bli frisk, Gud, vil jeg ofre en av mine turtelduer til Deg i morgen." Og datteren ble frisk, så han gjorde det.

text:
And by and by his wife got sick. So he made a promise to sacrifice the other turtledove. And then his wife gets well. And then another sick spell hit their home, and he didn't have anything to entertain the tourists by. And his begging was very poorly then. And he went outside to pray, and he said, "Lord, I only have one more thing, and that is my lamb."
Now, you've seen the blind being led by a dog. They train them for that purpose. In those days they had a lamb that led the blind. And he said, "Lord, if You will heal this case for me and let my little one live, I will sacrifice to You my lamb. It's all I've got."
And the next day the child was well. And he set on his road to sacrifice. The priest said to him, "Blind Bartimaeus, where goest thou?"
He said, "Oh, priest, I am going up to the altar to sacrifice my lamb, that I promised God I would do."
"Oh," he said, "blind Bartimaeus, you cannot give that lamb." Said, "I will give you some money, and you buy a lamb."
He said, "Oh, priest, I never promised God a lamb; I promised God this lamb." There's a lesson there. And I hope God teaches it to us.
text_no:
Etter hvert ble hans kone syk. Han lovet å ofre den siste turtelduen. Så ble kona frisk igjen. Deretter rammet nok en sykdom familien, og han hadde ingenting igjen å underholde turistene med. Tiggingen gikk dårlig. Han gikk ut for å be og sa: "Herre, jeg har bare én ting igjen, og det er mitt lam."
Du har kanskje sett blinde bli ledet av en hund. De trenes til det formålet. På den tiden hadde de et lam som ledet de blinde. Han sa: "Herre, hvis Du vil helbrede dette tilfellet for meg og la mitt lille barn leve, vil jeg ofre mitt lam til Deg. Det er alt jeg har."
Dagen etter var barnet friskt. Han la ut på veien for å ofre. Presten sa til ham: "Blinde Bartimeus, hvor går du?"
Han svarte: "Å, prest, jeg går til alteret for å ofre mitt lam, slik jeg lovet Gud."
"Å," sa presten, "blinde Bartimeus, du kan ikke ofre det lammet. Jeg gir deg noen penger, og du kan kjøpe et lam."
Han sa: "Å, prest, jeg lovet Gud ikke bare et lam; jeg lovet Gud dette lammet." Det ligger en lærdom i dette, og jeg håper Gud lærer oss den.

text:
And he said, "Blind Bartimaeus, thou cannot sacrifice that lamb. That lamb is your eyes."
He said, "Oh, priest of God, God will provide a lamb for blind Bartimaeus' eyes, if I keep my promise to Him." And on this cold October day while he drooped in rags God had provided a Lamb for blind Bartimaeus' eyes.
And may I say this, my beloved friends, that same Lamb is provided for your eyes tonight, and for your sickness. It's God's provided Lamb to take away your sins and your sickness. And someday you've got to stand in the presence of God, to give an account of this life that you've been permitted to live.
text_no:
Han sa, "Blinde Bartimeus, du kan ikke ofre det lammet. Det lammet er dine øyne." 
Bartimeus svarte, "Å, Guds prest, Gud vil sørge for et lam til blinde Bartimeus' øyne hvis jeg holder løftet mitt til Ham." Og på denne kalde oktoberdagen, mens han sto i filler, hadde Gud sørget for et Lam til blinde Bartimeus' øyne.
Og la meg si dette, mine kjære venner: Det samme Lammet er gitt for dine øyne i kveld og for din sykdom. Det er Guds utvalgte Lam for å ta bort dine synder og din sykdom. En dag må du stå i Guds nærvær og avgi regnskap for dette livet som du har fått lov til å leve.

text:
Not long ago there was a great man who crossed through the middle eastern countries, and his name was Daniel Curry. He was a famous revivalist. It's been fifty some odd years. And Daniel Curry, one night he said he had a dream. And he dreamed that he died and was taken up to heaven. And when he got to the gate of heaven, the opener of the gate came---the gatekeeper---and he said, "Who art thou that is approaching?"
He said, "I am Daniel Curry. I am the evangelist who won thousands of souls in the earth for heaven. I have come to take my place and receive my mansion."
Said, "Just a moment. I shall look on the book." And he went and looked at the book. And when he come back, he said, "I am sorry, Mr. Curry, but I do not find your name on the book."
"Oh," he said, "surely you're wrong. Go look again."
And he did the second time, and he said, "Your name is not on the book. Therefore I cannot open these gates to you. You have no name to check."
"Oh," he said, "what must I do?"
He said, "Sir, you might appeal your case to the great white throne judgment, if you care to."
"Well," he said, "I have no choice. I'll have to do that."
text_no:
For ikke lenge siden var det en stor mann som reiste gjennom Midtøsten, og navnet hans var Daniel Curry. Han var en kjent vekkelsespredikant. Dette var for omkring femti år siden. En natt fortalte Daniel Curry at han hadde en drøm. I drømmen døde han og ble tatt opp til himmelen. Da han kom til himmelens port, kom portvokteren og spurte: "Hvem er du som nærmer deg?"
Han svarte, "Jeg er Daniel Curry. Jeg er evangelisten som vant tusenvis av sjeler på jorden for himmelen. Jeg har kommet for å innta min plass og motta min bolig."
Portvokteren sa, "Et øyeblikk, jeg skal sjekke boken." Han sjekket boken og da han kom tilbake, sa han, "Jeg beklager, herr Curry, men jeg finner ikke navnet ditt i boken."
"Oh," svarte Daniel, "du må ta feil. Sjekk igjen."
Portvokteren sjekket for andre gang og sa, "Ditt navn står ikke i boken. Derfor kan jeg ikke åpne disse portene for deg. Du har ikke noe navn å vise til."
"Oh," spurte han, "hva skal jeg gjøre?"
Portvokteren svarte, "Herr, du kan appellere saken din til den store hvite trone-dommen, dersom du ønsker det."
"Vel," sa Daniel, "jeg har ikke noe valg. Jeg må gjøre det."

text:
And he said it seemed like he was taken through space for a long while. And it begin to get lighter, lighter, lighter. No certain place it was coming from. And he begin to slow up, and said, then all of a sudden he came to a stop, and there was the brightest light he had ever seen in all his life. It was thousand times brighter than the midday sun. And he said he heard a voice saying, "Daniel Curry, did you ever steal anything when you were on earth? You are here at My judgment seat."
And he said, "I thought I'd been an honest man." But said, "In the presence of that light I realized that there was some things I had pulled, little shady deals. I didn't think about it when I was on earth, but in the presence of that light…" Then he said, "Yes, Lord, I have stole."
He said, "Daniel Curry, did you ever tell a lie when you were in your life?"
Said, "I thought at least I'd been an honest man." But said, "In the presence of that light, I seen a lot of things that I had told that wasn't right." He said, "Lord, I have told lies."
Then said, "The voice come the third time, and said, 'Daniel Curry, was you perfect when you were on earth in your life?'"
He said, "No, Lord, I wasn't perfect." And said, "My bones seemed like they were coming apart, and I was listening for that great once blast, saying, 'Depart from me into outer darkness.'"
text_no:
Han sa at det virket som om han ble ført gjennom verdensrommet i lang tid. Det begynte å bli lysere, lysere og lysere uten at lyset kom fra et bestemt sted. Han begynte å roe ned og sa at plutselig stoppet han, og der var det klareste lyset han noensinne hadde sett. Det var tusen ganger sterkere enn middagssolen. Han hørte en stemme si: "Daniel Curry, stjal du noensinne noe da du var på jorden? Du er nå ved Min domstol."
Han svarte: "Jeg trodde jeg hadde vært en ærlig mann." Men i lysets nærvær innså han at det var noen små skyggefulle saker han hadde gjort. Han hadde ikke tenkt på det da han var på jorden, men i lysets nærvær... Så sa han: "Ja, Herre, jeg har stjålet."
Stemmen spurte: "Daniel Curry, løy du noen gang i løpet av ditt liv?"
Han trodde i det minste at han hadde vært ærlig. Men i lysets nærvær så han mange ting han hadde sagt som ikke var riktige. Han sa: "Herre, jeg har løyet."
Stemmen kom for tredje gang og spurte: "Daniel Curry, var du perfekt i ditt liv på jorden?"
Han svarte: "Nei, Herre, jeg var ikke perfekt." Og han sa at det føltes som om beina hans holdt på å falle fra hverandre, mens han ventet på den store dommen, som ville si: "Gå bort fra Meg, inn i det ytterste mørket."

text:
And said, "Just as I was getting ready to hear that great eternal separation word," said, "I heard the sweetest voice I ever heard in my life." Said, "Never did I ever hear a voice like that. No mother could speak words like that." Said, "I turned, and when I did," he said, "I saw the sweetest face I ever saw in all my life." Said, "No mother's face could look like that."
And said, "He walked up to me and put His arm around me and said, 'Father, no, Daniel Curry wasn't perfect on earth in his life. But in his life he stood for Me on the earth. And now in heaven, I will stand for him. Let all of his sins be put on My back.'"
So that's what I speak for, that's what I try to persuade others for. How you'll want Him to say, "Well done." I wonder who's standing for you tonight, if your life should be called. Could you stand the judgment of the white throne? Just think of it right quick while we bow our heads just a moment.
text_no:
Han sa: "Akkurat da jeg var i ferd med å høre det store ordet om evig separasjon, hørte jeg den søteste stemmen jeg noen gang har hørt i mitt liv." Han fortsatte: "Aldri har jeg hørt en slik stemme før. Ingen mor kunne snakke slik." Han sa videre: "Jeg snudde meg, og da jeg gjorde det, sa han, 'så jeg det søteste ansiktet jeg noen gang har sett.' Ingen mors ansikt kunne se slik ut."
Han sa: "Han kom bort til meg, la armen rundt meg og sa, 'Far, nei, Daniel Curry var ikke perfekt på jorden i sitt liv. Men på jorden stod han for Meg. Og nå i himmelen, vil Jeg stå for ham. La alle hans synder bli lagt på Min rygg.'"
Det er derfor jeg taler, og det er derfor jeg prøver å overbevise andre. Hvor sterkt vil du ikke ønske at Han sier: 'Vel gjort.' Jeg undrer på hvem som står for deg i kveld, hvis du skulle bli kalt hjem. Kan du bestå dommen for den hvite tronen? Tenk på det et øyeblikk mens vi bøyer hodene våre i bønn.

text:
And if the organist will be so kind to come to the organ and play a Bible tune if you will. With our heads bowed, every eye closed, and those who know how to pray…
This may be the moment when some precious soul finds its eternal destination. And the last knock may be knocked at the door of the heart of a person here. "My Spirit will not always strive with man." So it behooves us to pray, Christians. And now you're saying… I'm going to ask this to these sinners, the one that knows that he could not have someone to stand for him: Do you want Him to stand for you? If you do, would you just raise your hands to Him at this time, and say, "God, this is my hand as a sign. I want Jesus to stand for me in the hour of my death."
God bless you. God bless you, you. God bless you, lady. God bless you, sir. God bless you. That's right. Just keep your hands up. God bless you. You say, "Brother Branham, does that do something?" Yes. God bless you, little boy. God bless you, lady. That's right. Someone else out on the bottom floor? God bless you over there. Someone who hasn't put up their hand, would you put up your hand right away and say, "Jesus, I'm raising my hand." God bless you back there, girl. [Blank spot on tape.]
text_no:
Kunne organisten være så vennlig å sette seg ved orgelet og spille en salme? Med bøyd hode og lukkede øyne, til de som vet hvordan man ber...
Dette kan være øyeblikket når en sjel finner sin evige bestemmelse. Kanskje er dette siste gang det bankes på hjertets dør til en person her. "Min Ånd skal ikke for alltid kjempe med mennesket." Derfor må vi våkne opp og be, kristne. Nå sier dere... Jeg vil stille dette spørsmålet til synderne her, de som vet at de ikke har noen som kan stå for dem: Vil du at Han skal stå for deg? Hvis ja, løft hånden mot Ham nå, og si: "Gud, dette er min hånd som et tegn. Jeg ønsker at Jesus skal stå for meg i min dødstime."
Gud velsigne deg. Gud velsigne deg, deg. Gud velsigne deg, dame. Gud velsigne deg, herre. Gud velsigne deg. Det er riktig. Bare hold hendene oppe. Gud velsigne deg. Du spør: "Bror Branham, betyr dette noe?" Ja. Gud velsigne deg, lille gutt. Gud velsigne deg, dame. Det er riktig. Er det noen andre på gulvet foran? Gud velsigne deg der borte. Er det noen som ennå ikke har løftet hånden sin? Løft hånden nå og si: "Jesus, jeg løfter hånden min." Gud velsigne deg der bak, jente. [Tomt område på lydbånd.]

text:
"I know that I have nothing I could offer, but all that I have I give." Back and to my left, would you raise your hands and… "Stand for me, Jesus." Oh, my. There's hands, God bless you everywhere. In the balconies to the center, raise your hands and say, "God be merciful to me right now. I'm making my stand for You, Christ. You will stand for me at that day." Just hands everywhere. God bless you. Oh, how the Holy Spirit is moving in the building.
I'm going to ask you something, while the rest of them are praying. You who wants to hear that voice when some morning the fog begins to come into the room, and the doctor's left and said there's not a hope. You feel your pulse coming up your sleeve, and you know the time has come; you're leaving. Do you want Him to stand there and bear you across Jordan, and your loved ones [unclear words] not knowing when He will break.
If you want to be sincere, and want to prove to God that you want Him to put your name on the Lamb's book of life, and to stand for you on that day, will you quietly stand to your feet while we have prayer? Everyone that wants to make that stand tonight for Christ, would you just stand. On the bottom floor and in the balconies on every side, would you who raised up your hand, stand up just a moment now. You make your stand. That's it. God bless you. That's right. Everywhere.
text_no:
"Jeg vet at jeg ikke har noe å tilby, men alt jeg har gir jeg." Der bak og til venstre, kan du rekke opp hånden og ... "Stå opp for meg, Jesus." Å, Gud velsigne deg. Hender overalt. I balkongene, i midten, løft hendene og si: "Gud, vær meg nådig nå. Jeg tar min stand for Deg, Kristus. Du vil stå for meg på den dagen." Bare hender overalt. Gud velsigne dere. Å, hvordan Den Hellige Ånd beveger seg i bygningen.
Jeg vil spørre dere om noe mens resten ber. Dere som vil høre den stemmen når en morgen tåken begynner å komme inn i rommet, og legen har dratt og sagt at det ikke er noe håp. Du føler pulsen komme opp armen din, og du vet tiden er inne; du skal dra. Vil du at Han skal stå der og bære deg over Jordan, og du er sammen med dine kjære, uviss om når Han vil komme.
Hvis du vil være oppriktig og ønsker å bevise for Gud at du vil at Han skal skrive ditt navn i Lammets bok og stå for deg på den dagen, vil du stille reise deg mens vi ber? Alle som vil ta denne stansen for Kristus i kveld, vær så snill å reise dere. På nederste etasje og i balkongene på alle sider, dere som rakte opp hånden, stå opp et øyeblikk nå. Ta din stand. Der er det. Gud velsigne deg. Det er riktig. Overalt.

text:
Just stand right up and say, "I'm making my stand before these people."
Jesus said, "If you're ashamed of Me… (Now, just remain standing.) If you're ashamed of Me before men, I will be ashamed of you before My Father. But he that will confess Me before men, him will I confess before My Father." Oh, friends, we're going to need it. We're coming right to it. It may be before morning, but you've got to stand.
Let another host stand up, will you, someone who'll say, "I will take my stand tonight." God bless you, sir. Someone else stand up while we offer prayer in the presence of God. God bless you, brother. God bless you. Someone else? God bless you. God bless you, lady. God bless you. Will you take your stand, say, "God, I will stand for Christ now. And, O Christ, You promised me that You'd stand for me on that day."
text_no:
